coredata.dc:description: Nano-structured semiconductors, a new class of particles emerged as a good candidate for applications, especially in electronics, optoelectronics and biology because they can bridge the gap between the bulk and molecular levels. They have size-tunable optical and electronic properties, very high available surface area and optical transparency. They can be synthesized with a wide variety of chemical compositions using capping ligands to control size without aggregation and dispersed in solvents for room-temperature deposition. They have attracted remarkable interest in both basic scientific research and in industrial development in the area of optoelectronics due to ease of processing, low cost. On the other hand organic semiconductors are another class of promising materials for organic and hybrid electronics due to their low cost and flexibility. In this chapter basic aspects of synthesis and applications of nano-particles and organic semiconductors is presented. They have attracted remarkable interest in both basic scientific research and industrial applications. They can be used for a wide variety of applications, including solar cells (PV), organic field-effect transistors (OFETs), organic light emitting diodes (OLEDs) etc.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nano-structured semiconductors, a new class of particles emerged as a good candidate for applications, especially in electronics, optoelectronics and biology because they can bridge the gap between the bulk and molecular levels. They have size-tunable optical and electronic properties, very high available surface area and optical transparency. They can be synthesized with a wide variety of chemical compositions using capping ligands to control size without aggregation and dispersed in solvents for room-temperature deposition. They have attracted remarkable interest in both basic scientific research and in industrial development in the area of optoelectronics due to ease of processing, low cost. On the other hand organic semiconductors are another class of promising materials for organic and hybrid electronics due to their low cost and flexibility.

In this chapter basic aspects of synthesis and applications of nano-particles and organic semiconductors is presented. They have attracted remarkable interest in both basic scientific research and industrial applications. They can be used for a wide variety of applications, including solar cells (PV), organic field-effect transistors (OFETs), organic light emitting diodes (OLEDs) etc.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles are important for nanoscience and nanotechnology. Nanoparticles act as linkages between atomic materials or molecules and bulk materials. They have generally 1–100nm particle size. They have been produced for different sectors of economic activity because they have importance for various biotechnological, pharmacological, and pure technological uses.

Different methods can be used for the synthesis of nanoparticles. Each method has some advantages beside disadvantages in comparison with others. We give some details for four different methods below.

It is the synthesis method by the injection of organometallic initiator in an inert atmosphere and in a high temperature solution environment. Heavy metals as cationic initiators and chalcogenide elements such as Se, Te, and S as anionic initiators are used. The solutions of the chalcogenides are prepared by dissolving them through surface active materials which can be used at high temperature and the reaction is carried out by mixing all reactants [1].
As shown in Fig. 24.1 , these reactions are carried out at very high temperatures such as 250–350°C [2–3].

In this synthesis method, micelles are formed by a secondary reaction on the crystal surface. By means of functionalizing the crystal surface by use of micelles, the shape and speed of growth can be controlled. Mulder and colleagues fabricated crystals with a micelle layer after synthesizing quantum nanocrystals (NCs) [1,4]. The main disadvantages of this method can be the low fluorescence quantum yields and disorders of crystal structure. These types of NCs are generally used in some biological applications.

In this method, two immiscible solvents are used during synthesis. The nucleation and crystal growth occurs at the interface. One phase is generally water and it includes a chalcogenide source, such as thiourea. The other phase is generally organic solvents such as toluene and includes organometallic source as a metal ion source, such as cadmium myristate, and a surfactant, such as oleic acid. The nucleation starts at the liquid–liquid interface, formed particles are surrounded by surfactant and stay in organic solvent. Over time the nanoparticles grow at interface. This method allows the control of particles growth and formation of fine crystals. So, it is one of the most widely used methods for colloidal NC synthesis [1,5].

In the solvothermal method, the synthesis is carried out at higher temperatures than the boiling point of solvent leading to high pressure in an autoclave. Fig. 24.2 shows a schematic view of an autoclave. In the case where water is used as the solvent in the reaction, the method is called hydrothermal, while it is called as solvothermal in the case of an organic solvent being used in the reaction. [1,6].

The advantage of this method is that the high pressure accelerates the dissolution of reactants which are not fully soluble in environmental conditions. The particle size control can be done by changing the ratio of the output material and solvent used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water Phase Synthesis Under Atmospheric Pressure
ce:section.ce:para: It possible to carry out the synthesis of colloidal NCs under atmospheric pressure. In this method all reactans are dissolved in water and reactions are carried out by refluxing the mixture. Fig. 24.3 shows a schematic presentation of such systems.

In synthesis, water-soluble salts such as perchlorate and chloride are generally used as metal initiators. Sulfur sources such as Na2S, thioacetamide, and thiourea are usually preferred. NaHSe and NaHTe, which are produced from elemental Se and Te by using NaBH4 salt, are used as well as components, such as H2Se and H2Te formed by decomposition of aluminum chalcogenides, to be the Se and Te source. The use of initiators such as H2Te is not limited to water alone, but can be used in organic solvents and even in ionic liquids. NaHTe can be used only in water. The synthetic method for obtaining water-soluble thiol-coated NCs is a process consisting of three steps and being performed in a single vessel.
1.
After dissolving the metal (M) salt in water, metal–thiol complexes form with the addition of a suitable thiol (surfactant) and then pH adjustment,
2.
Injection of the chalcogenide source into the metal/thiol solution completely passes through the inert gas (Ar, N2), and consequently the formation of the MeX (metal/thiol complex) initiator,
3.
It consists of steps such as nucleation and growth of NCs by heating under reflux or microwave.
Fig. 24.3 demonstrates NC synthesis in water by the production of hydrogen chalcogenide gas from aluminum chalcogenides.

A cationic initiator ligand (surfactant) is used in the synthesis of such NCs. As anionic initiators (Cd metal cation), chalcogen elements such as Te, Se, and S are usually used. There are various different kinds of synthesis route that can be used in the synthesis of these materials. The most important thing in synthesis is temperature control since the control of particle size is important. In addition, surfactants and solvents are also important, affecting the parameters for shape, size, and quality of the particles.
The surface active materials are used to wrap around the particles and they allow them to dissolve in water, disperse in organic solvents, and prevent the agglomerating.

Oleic acid is a fatty acid and can be obtained from many animal and vegetable sources. Due to the long aliphatic chain, oleic acid-coated nanoparticles can be easily dispersed in apolar organic solvents. Particles obtained with oleic acid have relatively narrow emission peaks compared to TOPO. However, in many biological applications, it is desirable to disperse the particles in water. So some water-soluble surfactants, such as 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and Thioglycolic acid (Fig. 24.6 ), are used to dissolve nanoparticles in water. Thiol groups anchor to the particle surface while carboxylic acid groups remain free leading to dissolving in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binary QDs (CdTe, CdSe, and CdS QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Anionic initiators (selenium, tellurium, or sulfur) are dissolved in TOPO or TOP. After the Cd source and the surfactant have sufficiently dissolved, the hot anionic initiator mixture is added and the reaction is initiated. The reaction occurs at a temperature as high as 300°C and under nitrogen atmosphere. Dimethylcadmium, a highly toxic material, and cadmium acetate are generally used as a cadmium precursor [8]. Talapin et al. synthesized CdTe nanoparticles via organometallic synthesis route. In this synthesis method dodecylamine and trioctylphosphine were used as surfactants and cadmium oxide as cadmium source [9].
They obtained highly fluorescent fine nanoparticles with this method. CdTe NCs can be also synthesized by using NaHTe obtained from Te powder [10].

Alloyed quantum dots (QDs) can be classified as homogeneous (a uniform internal structure) and a gradient structure. Alloyed QDs also can be classified as either ternary (three elements) or quarternary (four elements). Ternary alloys are formed by using two binary QDs such as M′A and M″A. For instance, to obtain (M′A) x (M″A)1−x or M′ x M″1−x A alloy QDs, two different cations (M′ and M″) and A as anion are commonly used. For example, Zn x Cd1−x Se is an alloy QD structure. Two different anions (A′ and A″) can also be used instead of two different metal cations in the synthesis. For example CdS x Se1−x structure consists of two different anions.

Quarternary alloys have the composition of M′ x M″1−x A′yA″1−y . They are chalcopyrite materials and CuIn x Ga1−x Se2 (CIGS) may be an example for this type of QDs. A ternary with a binary semiconductor can also yield a quartenary QD such as (CuInS2) x (ZnS)1−x
[11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ternary Quantum Dots (CdTeS, CdTeSe, CdSeS QDs)
ce:section.ce:para:
In syntheses using groups II–IV of the periodic table, NCs with structures of CdS x Se1−x
[12], Cd x Zn1−x S [13], Cd x Zn1−x Se [14,15], CdSe x Te1−x
[16], and HgSe x S1−x
[17] can be synthesized. These crystals are divided into two groups. In the first group there are two chalcogen elements and one metal element, while in the second group there are two metals and a chalcogen element (Cd x Zn1−x S, Cd x Zn1−x Se).

Hot-injection synthesis method can be used for CdSeS type of alloyed QDs. This method is also known as organometallic high-temperature method. These crystals can be synthesized by a simple method [18]. Microvawe irradiation is also used for synthesis. Qian et al. synthesized alloyed CdSeS QDs using a mixture of oxygen-free NaHSe solution and CdCl2/3-MPA via microwave irradiation.

Another method for obtaining these crystals is the solvothermal synthesis route. For this method, autoclaves have used high temperature and pressure-resistant containers. Very low dissolving or insoluble materials are soluble under high temperature and pressure. By this method, CdSeS NCs with very different morphology and structure can be produced.

In one study, for example, CdSeS synthesis was successfully performed in ethylenediamine using CdC2O4 and S powder and corresponding elemental Se [19,20]. The properties of alloyed QDs vary directly with the composition, and even with the proportions of the chalcogen elements. For example, the emission energies of CdTeSe alloyed QDs are far outside of the self-forming binary structures (CdTe and CdSe). Thus, this effect (Te/Se) varies with the ratio of the combined chalcogen elements. Nilanka P. Gurusinghe et al. have obtained alloyed CdTeS QDs with different structure by organometallic synthesis method by changing Te and S ratio [18,21].

Many various synthesis of gold nanoparticles were reported. Generally, gold nanoparticles are produced with wet chemical method. The citrate reduction is the well-known method for obtaining Au nanoparticles. Solution of chloroauric acid (H[AuCl4]) is mixed with a small amount of reducing agent, usually sodium citrate, Au3+ ions are reduced to gold atoms by the reaction. Too many gold atoms form and at the same time the citrate ions act as a capping agent to stabilize gold nanoparticles. Some of stabilizing agents such as organic ligands have also been used to prevent aggregating by binding to the surface of the particles [22,23]. This method is also known as the Turkevich method, being an old and the most classic process. It allows to produce monodispersed spherical gold nanoparticles. This synthesis route is very sensitive to pH, the ionic strength of the medium, and the presence of other organic materials. To produce larger gold nanoparticles, less sodium citrate should be added [22,23].

Brust and Schiffrin proposed another method which allows to obtain high quality materials with narrow size distributions. The Brust process of gold nanoparticle synthesis is an important technique for obtaining thiol-stabilized nanoparticles. The obtained gold nanoparticles in organic liquids are normally not soluble in water. In this process, the reaction of a chlorauric acid solution with tetraoctylammonium bromide solution dissolved in toluene by using sodium borohydride as an anticoagulant and a reducing agent gives extremely small nanoparticles, around 2–6nm.
Ag NPs are also interesting materials. Three main approaches to the synthesis of silver nanoparticles are summarized below.

In physical processes, there are two approaches named evaporation-condensation and laser ablation. The absence of solvent pollution and the uniformity of the sizes of the nanoparticles are among the greatest advantages of physical processes over chemical syntheses. In the evaporation method, a tube furnace is used under atmospheric pressure. The source is vaporized into a carrier gas at the center of the silver furnace as melt. Synthesis of many nanomaterials such as Ag, Au, PbS, and fullerene is accomplished by using this technique. In these syntheses it is important that the temperature is steady and the energy required to increase the temperature around the material is great. Codimensional particles are obtained with homogeneous distribution by this method.

Silver NCs are also synthesized by laser ablation method from metallic bromide materials in solution. The advantage of this method is that there are no chemical reagents in the solution media to prepare metal colloids. The key parameters that must be considered are the laser wavelength, the application time of the laser pulses, the influence of the laser, and the influence of the liquid environment in the presence and absence of surfactant [24–34].

The most common species of silver nanoparticle synthesis is based on chemical reduction with organic and inorganic reducing reagents. In general, three main components: (1) metalic precursors; (2) reduction agents; and (3) stabilizing/capping agents are needed for this method. Reduction agents such as sodium citrate, ascorbate, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), elemental hydrogen, polyol process, tollens reagent, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and poly (ethylene glycol)-block copolymers are used. Silver nanoparticles with a few nanometers are obtained by reducing Ag+ ions in solution [35–37]. It is important to use surfactant for surface bonding and surface protection [38]. In these syntheses, nucleation at the beginning and sufficient growth of the nucleus can be achieved by adjusting the reaction parameters such as reaction temperature, pH, precursors, reduction agents (i.e., NaBH4, ethylene glycol, glucose), and stabilizing agents. Turkevich and Brust methods are also other methods for chemical synthesis of Ag NPs as mentioned for synthesis of Au NPs in a previous section. Tollens method, in which the particle size can be controlled in a single step, is also known. The basic Tollens reaction involves the reduction of Ag(NH3)2+ (aq), by an aldehyde [39]. According to the Tollens process, Ag+ ions form Ag NPs ranging in size from 50 to 200nm through ammonia and saccharides. In the presence of ammonia ions, a strong complex is formed with silver ions. Particle formation occurs through ammonia concentration and reduction [34,40].

Development of ceramic nanoparticles such as silica, alumina, titania, zirconia, silicon nitride, silicon carbide with improved properties has been studied in several. Silica nanoparticles have also been widely used for biomedical applications, such as in vivo lifetime of drugs and macromolecules, high payload targeted delivery, theranostic, and new therapies or diagnostics due to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, and scalable synthetic availability. Therefore, it is important to control silica particle size, porosity, crystallinity, and shape to tune the nanostructure, and the ability to adapt these properties for these particles makes these particles the desired materials for applications.

Silica particles can be extracted from natural sources but they contain impurities. Synthetic silica nanoparticles forms are colloidal silica, silica gels, pyrogenic silica, and precipitated silica and they can be produced as purely powder and amorphous forms. Natural mineral silica forms such as quartz, tridymite, cristobalite are also in crystalline forms [41,42].

There are two main approaches, top-down and bottom-up, to synthesis methods of silica particles [43–44]. Top-down approach is reducing the dimension of the original size by different special size reduction techniques and it is called a physical approach. According to the bottom-up or chemical approach, silica nanoparticles have been produced from atomic or molecular scale. Sol–gel process, reverse microemulsion, and flame synthesis can be described to be bottom-up or chemical synthesis of silica nanoparticles [41].

The sol–gel process is widely and frequently used techniques for preparation of Si NPs. This method allows to control the particle size, size distribution and morphology based on reaction parameters. It is possible to produce silica, glass and ceramic materials purely and homogeneously in the sol–gel process. It involves colloidal suspension (sol) and gelation to form a system in continuous liquid phase (gel) [45,46]. The precursors of the sol–gel process have two classes namely inorganic precursors (chlorides, nitrates, sulfides, etc.) and alkoxide precursors. Metal (Me) alkoxides (Me(OR)4) such as tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, Si(OC2H5)4) in the presence of mineral acid (e.g., HCl) or base (e.g., NH3) as catalyst can be used for synthesis. The sol is a liquid in which colloidal particles are dispersed and a gel is an interconnected rigid network with pores of submicron dimensions and polymeric chains [41,47]. The properties of materials depend on the type of catalyst. For example, if an acid is used catalyst, linear polymers form or if a base is used as catalyst, synthesis results in branched clusters (Fig. 24.7 ) [48].

Carbon nanodots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials and they were first prepared from purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes. They have particle sizes of 10nm. They have received increasing attention for their synthesis and applications. One of the synthesis methods is the electrochemical approach. C-dots have been prepared using various bulk carbon materials as precursors by this method. For example, CQDs can be prepared from different small molecular alcohols by using the electrochemical carbonization method [49–51]. Zhou et al. also reported electrochemical synthesis of C-dots when they grew multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) from scrolled graphene by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [52]. Zhao et al. reported electrochemically synthesis of C-dots. In this work, oxidizing a graphitic column electrode against a saturated calomel electrode with a Pt wire counter electrode is used in a NaH2PO4 aqueous solution [53]. However the size-controllability of C-dots is difficult and complicated with traditional electrochemical methods [54–55].

Laser ablation technique is also used for C-dot synthesis. According to the method of Sun et al., laser ablation is applied to a carbon target in the presence of argon as a carrier gas and water vapor at 900°C. After 12hours of refluxing in the HNO3 solution, the surface is coated with various types of polyethylene glycol and then bright luminescence-emitting C-dot nanoparticles [51,56–57].

Microwave irradiation method is a low cost and fast method which is cumulative with being an important process in the field of synthetic chemistry [51]. Materials such as sucrose or diethylene glycol (DEG) can be used as carbon source. Liu et al. have successfully synthesized highly fluorescent C-dots with citric acid and various amine molecules using microwave-mediated pyrolysis [58]. In this synthesis, amine molecules act as doping initiators and surfactants. The resulting C-dots are highly suitable for biomedical applications and have high biocompatibility [59].

Hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment is an environmentally friendly, low cost, and harmless to the environment production technique for most nanoparticle synthesis. Natural initiators such as glucose [60], citric acid [61], chitosan [62], banana juice [63], and protein [49] can be used as organic starters. For example, Mohapatra et al. synthesized CQDs with very high fluorescence emission by using orange juice. In the solvothermal process, extraction is carried out with an organic solvent [64–65].

Until recently, material technology and science dealt with systems in the range of micrometers. However, a new approach of modern science, called nanotechnology and nanoscience, evolved to build machines and devices at the molecular range in the past few decades [66–67]. This new direction is an interdisciplinary area and an incredibly broad field to create new materials and unique products. Nanotechnology and nanoscience involve the ability to see and to control the materials as small as atoms and molecules, individually. Also, it allows to design and to produce new materials with improved feature such as lighter weight, higher strength, or greater chemical resistance/reactivity, by controlling size and shape of nanomaterials. Nanoscale materials produced with the help of nanotechnology find a wide range of application areas such as biotechnologies, tissue engineering, electronic and mechanic devices, computer systems, optical products etc. nowadays. The growing numbers of nanoscale materials in global markets are the most economical and important factors of nanotechnology today and tomorrow.

In electronics, the devices and functional materials based on nanoscale materials are seen as natural by the researchers and engineers. Nanoscale materials, such as nanofibers (NFs), nanotubes (NTs), NCs, nanowires (NWs) and quantum wires (QWs), QDs, polymers, small-molecules, and nanocomposites can be used in new device applications [68]. Nanoscale materials have been coming to increasing attention in electronic applications such solar cells, field effect transistors (FETs), light-emitting diodes (LEDs), chemical and biological sensors, and lasers etc. Especially, organic materials have sustained stable progress in these fields for the last few years. Organic materials used can be divided into two main classes: polymers and organic small molecules. The research on these topics has been accelerated owing to their potential for low cost, flexible electronics with large area, adjustable electrical and physical properties, controllable resistance and conductivity, and fabrication without intense and stringent environment [69,70]. Today, there are many commercialized products based on organic materials in global markets and these products are generally regarded as the most competitive member of new generation electronics. Therefore, polymers and small molecules have been widely researched to improve their electrical properties [71]. This section is structured to examine the properties of polymers and organic small molecules, and their synthesis methods.

Traditional polymers in their pure (undoped) state are utilized as insulators and dielectrics in electronic applications. With the invention of the conductive polymer in 1977 by Alan G. MacDiarmid, Hideki Shirakawa, and Alan J. Heeger et al., the conventional concept has been changed and the conductive polymers have received increasing attention from both academic and industrial areas [72–73]. Until the beginning of the 1980s, a series of conductive polymers, involving polyacetylene (PAc), polyaniline (PANI), and polypyrrole (PPy), were reported. After that, conductive polymers have played a key role in the electronic industry and they seem to be competitive for a long time.

Intrinsic conductive polymers are polymers that have a conjugated molecular backbone. The conjugation can be due to π electrons delocalization or due to ingredients doped to the polymer chain. Table 24.1 summarizes some representative conjugating polymers, their main chains, and their conductivities. Also, an overview of conductivity range of CPs is given in Fig. 24.8
[74].

As mentioned above, CPs have a series of consecutive double and single bonds, creating a conjugated backbone (seen in Table 24.1), which gives an increase in conductivity [75]. The overlapping of the conjugated π electrons in the backbone allows the electrons to delocalize into conduction bands through the backbone [76]. This develops a metallic behavior. However, this idealized case is unstable due to the occurrence of an energy gap [72]. The dopant ions introduced to the structure are the key to removing the energy gap and to stabilizing the backbone of the polymer in an oxidized state [77]. These dopant ions behave as extra electrons to neutralize the charge by accepting or donating electrons [78]. The doping process of conjugated polymers includes both oxidation and reduction processes and according to desired CPs, the polymer can be p- or n-doped both chemically or electrochemically [79]. In the p-doping of CPs, the main chain is partially oxidized with a counteranion to preserve the electrical neutrality. It results in the increasing free carrier concentration of holes in the main chain that makes the polymer p-type conductive. Congruently, the main chain is partially reduced with countercations to preserve the electrical neutrality in the n-doping of CPs. It results in the increase of electrons that make the polymer n-type conductive [80]. This tunable conductivity makes the CPs useful materials for electronic application. Previously, numerous researchers examined the electrical properties of some different CPs and then they studied the parameters that affect their electrical properties [81–84]. For example, Tanaka et al. investigated the temperature dependence of electrical properties of doped PAc [85]. They showed that the conductivity of PAc is highly dependent on the temperature. If we summarize, some main parameters effect the conductivity of CPs, as follows [86–88]:
•
Presence, and type of doping materials.
•
Doping degree.
•
Temperature.
•
Structure.
•
Density of charge carrier etc.

The optical properties of materials give information about their electronic structure, because the electromagnetic field of light wave create the response from all charges, namely free or bound electrons, ions, plasmons, polarons, polar phonos, etc. This response is generally described by the dielectric function, which is related to band structure and refractive index of materials. The dielectric function is basically formulized as follows [89]:
