where ε is the complex dielectric function and ε(ω) is the dielectric constant of the material as a function of angular frequency ω. The determination of the optical properties from the dielectric function or the conductivity, and vice versa, is possible by using Maxwell equation.

The optical properties of CPs have been investigated due to their potential use in solar cells, FETs, and nanophotonic devices, such as photodetectors, photosensors, and photonic lasers [90–93]. In literature, there are many studies focused on synthesis of new CPs, to characterize their properties and to enhance their optical properties. Turac et al. reported a study to synthesize a new polythiophene derivative by electrochemical oxidative polymerization of 2,5-di(thiophen-2-yl)-1-(4-(thiophen-3-yl)phenyl)-1-H-pyrrole (TTPP) and to determine its optoelectrochemical properties [94]. In optoelectronics, controlling of structural parameters affects the mode density, which means the number of optical modes in the device per unit frequency, because it affects the interaction between the light and electronic excitations in CPs [95]. In many studies, researchers have used the structural control to improve optical properties of CPs. It was reported that the 1D structured materials are more convenient to produce photosensor and photodedector or photonic wire lasers [91,96] (Fig. 24.9 shows the schematic drawing of 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D dimensionality). Deutsch et al. reported that 3D structured photonic crystals can be obtained from CP poly(p-phenylenevinylene) (PPV) and the periodic structure of crystal improved the optoelectronic properties of PPV [95]. In addition, interpolymer and intrapolymer chain interactions coupled to molecular vibration modes effect the properties of CPs. Therefore, different methods have been carried out to enhance the optical properties in literature [97]. Shao et al. studied the isotopic effects of deuterium substitution on the physical, structural, and electronical properties of regioregular poly(3-hexylthiophene)s and they showed that deuteration of poly(3-hexylthiophene) results in different optoelectronic properties [98].

Before introducing the magnetic properties of CPs, magnetism must be explained shortly. All materials behave quite differently in the presence of an external magnetic field and according to their behavior, they can be classified into three main groups: diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic [99]. Diamagnetism follows the Lenz’s Law and diamagnetic materials exhibit a weak and negative magnetic susceptibility which means that they magnetize to oppose the applied magnetic field. In the other class, paramagnetic materials have some unpaired electrons and in the external magnetic field, realignment of electron paths causes paramagnetism. Paramagnetics magnetize in a parallel direction to the applied magnetic field. Organic molecules that contain an unpaired electron in doublet ground state exhibit paramagnetism. Ferromagnetic materials contain unpaired electrons and they show atomic magnetic moment. In ferromagnetism, magnetic susceptibility is very high and they magnetize in a parallel direction to the applied magnetic field.

There are many studies on the investigation of magnetic properties of CPs such as PAc [100–101], PPy [102], PANI [103–104], etc. since the magnetic susceptibility of CPs identifies the charge carrier species, the density of states (DOS) at the Fermi level and the unpaired spins. For example, Scott et al. reported that the doped PPy shows the electrical conductivity through bipolarons without Pauli-like susceptibility [105]. Another study reported that Pauli susceptibility or finite DOS at the Fermi energy exists at high doped states in PTh and this report studied the temperature dependence of magnetic susceptibility of PTh [106]. In addition, PAc, PPy, PANI as the highest conductive polymers are reported to possess finite DOS at the Fermi level. It is known that possessing the Fermi level in the conduction band results in Pauli susceptibility and enables metallic conduction [107]. According to reported studies, magnetic properties of CPs are strongly dependent on temperature, presence and type of doping materials, doping degree, and magnetic field similar to other properties of CPs [108].

The mechanic properties of polymers consist of the viscoelasticity, flexibility, elastomericity, tensile/compressive/shear, and torsional strength etc. [109]. For the CPs, the combination of these delightful properties with electrical conductivity bring to us the wearable and flexible electronic technologies. Traditional CPs show mechanical flexibility but limited elasticity due to their rigid conjugated backbone [110]. Therefore, the design of new CPs with desirable features and the improving of mechanical properties of new and present CPs is one of the most important parameters for future electronic application, such as flexible touch screens and displays or wearable biomonitoring devices. In recent years, various approaches have been reported in order to develop new CPs and to improve their mechanical properties. One of the most popular techniques is the blending of CPs with different materials such as carbonaceous materials [111], graphene [112–113], and metal nanoparticles [114]. Lin et al. studied the mechanical and electrical properties of PANI composite films incorporated with highly aligned MWCNTs. It was found that resulting composite films are flexible and the electrical conductivity remained almost same while the films are flexing. They showed that this film can be used as flexible electrode materials [115]. In a different study, De et al. investigated the properties of composite films of single-walled nanotubes blended with PEDOT:PSS and it was reported that the composite thin films are flexible and electrochemically stable. Analogously to the study of Lin et al., this CPs composite film displays no degradation of electrical conductivity while flexing [116]. In addition, the effects of parameters on the mechanical properties of CPs have been widely studied to improve their special features. Satoh et al. investigated the mechanical properties of PPy films polymerized under various conditions. They reported that both mechanical and electrical properties of prepared PPy films were affected by polymerization conditions [117]. In a different study on PPy CP, Cuenot et al. investigated the effect of nanotubes outer diameter and thickness of PPy nanotubes on the elastic tensile modulus and they showed that the elastic modulus strongly increases when the outer diameter or thickness of PPy nanotubes decreases [118]. As a summary, there are some main parameters that affect the magnetic properties of CPs, as follows:
•
Preparation conditions, i.e., preparation method, materials used, impurities of precursors and solvents [117],
•
Polymerizing and annealing temperature [119],
•
Structure depending on the application area (nanofibers, thin films, etc.) [120–121],
•
Ingredients (for composite materials) [122].

The wettability is identified by contact angle (CA) measurements. For a droplet of a liquid chosen on a surface, the CA is assumed to be two types of surface tension at the interface between the liquid and the surface. If the CA is less than 90°, the surface identifies as hydrophilic; whereas if the CA is higher than 90°, the surface identifies as hydrophobic [123]. The surface wettability has an important role on various applications involving self-cleaning surfaces for aeronautics, smart windows, and automotive industry, waterproof textiles, antifingerprint or antireflective surfaces for electronic and optical devices, controlled drug delivery, antibacterial adhesion, microfluidics, and separation technologies [90,124]. Herewith, controlling of the surface wettability has been of some attention as a scientific topic in electronic devices. CPs allow to control the surface or films wettability by changing the polymerization core and doping ions (hydrophobic/hydrophilic) used or by incorporating with substituents to increase/decrease the hydrophobicity of surface or for different functionalities needed [125]. In literature, there are various studies on the wettability properties of CPs. It was reported that tuning the chemical composition of CPs allow to have switchable surface with superhydrophobic and superhydrophilic features [126]. For example, Zhu et al. showed that the doping of perfluorooctane sulfonic acid induced the superhydrophobic properties of PANI [127]. Darmanin and Guittard reported that the hydrophobic surface of PPy can be induced by appending of hydrocarbon or fluorocarbon tails on pyrrole [128]. In a different study, hydrophobicity of the PPy and PEDOT surfaces is improved after the reaction with fluorinated alkyl thiols without any significant change to the surface morphologies [129].

CPs can be prepared by different techniques, namely chemical or electrochemical polymerization, photochemical pomerization, metathesis, plasma polymerization etc. depending on the desired properties of the CPs [130,131]. Among them, the most common techniques are mainly chemical and electrochemical polymerization [130]. These polymerization techniques are usually oxidative, but the reductive routes are also possible [132,133]. In this part, the most used methods will be discussed to synthesize the most common CPs in electronic applications.

Chemical polymerization consists of the chemical oxidative polymerization and metal-catalyzed coupling condensation. In a typical route of chemical oxidative polymerization, an oxidation agent (e.g., ferric chloride or ammonium persulfate) is dissolved with a monomer in a solvent at a certain temperature. The oxidation agent initiates the polymerization and the monomer oxidizes to radical cation. Two radical cations concatenate to a dimer. The resultant dimer oxides to a dimer radical cation and this permanency reaction constitutes the oligomer followed by formation of polymer chain [134]. This radical cation mechanism is still unknown, but it has been adopted by many researchers [135,136]. Chemical polymerization is recommended if the enlarged production scale is needed. The common CPs, such as PANI, PPy, are mostly prepared by chemical polymerization.

PANI can be produced by the chemical polymerization in acidic solution with potassium dichromate, hydrogen peroxide, or ammonium persulfate etc. as the oxidation agent [134,137]. Gomes and Oliveira have reported a classical experimental method to prepare doped PANI by using ammonium persulfate as an oxidation agent [138]. The detailed experimental method is as follows: 20mL of aniline was dissolved in 40mL of 1mol/L aqueous solution of HCl (hydrochloric acid) and the prepared solution was kept in a mixture of ice-salt at –10°C. At the same time, 12g of ammonium persulfate was added to 160mL of 1mol/L aqueous solution of HCl. The prepared acid solution of ammonium persulfate was dropped into the acid solution of aniline under stirring for 2h at –10°C. The precipitate was filtered, rinsed with aqueous solution of HCl and then dried at room temperature for 72h to obtain a doped state conductive PANI (PANI-HCl) powder. In addition to chemical polymerization in acidic medium, it has been reported that the PANI can be synthesized in aqueous solution by a similar method to that mentioned above without adding acid [139].

As one of the most common and stable CPs, PPy can also be produced by chemical polymerization with FeCl3 or ammonium persulfate as oxidation agent similar to PANI [140]. Also, it is well known that the electrical, morphological properties, and stability of PPy produced by this method depend on the reaction condition. Therefore, the experimental conditions (e.g., reactant stoichiometry, oxidation agent, concentration, temperature, solvent, time etc.) are important to prepare PPy with the desired properties [141]. There are several studies on the effects of parameters on the properties of produced conductive PPy. For example, Rapi et al. investigated the effect of polymerization term and temperatures on electrical conductivity of PPy [142]. They showed that the electrical conductivity of PPy can be improved at 0°C in water using a short-term polymerization with a low molar ratio of oxidant:pyrrole. Cavallaro et al. prepared PPy by chemical polymerization of 0.01mol/L of redistilled pyrrole with 600mL of 0.025mol/L FeCl3 solution in acetonitrile under stirring at 500r.p.m. in air atmosphere [143]. He et al. reported PPy powders having a conductivity of 43S/cm (for the compressed pellets) prepared with 0.18mol/L of pyrrole in a mixture of poly(vinylalcohol)-dodecyl-benzenesulfonic acid and 0.26mol/L of the same oxidation agent [144].

The electrochemical polymerization of CPs involves the direct oxidation of corresponding monomer by three different techniques, namely applying a constant current (galvanostatic), applying a constant potential (potentiostatic), and applying a sweeping potential (potentiodynamic) [145,146], hence, there is no need for use of an oxidation agent. Electrochemical polymerizations are performed by using a three electrode system involving the counter, reference, and working electrodes. During the electrochemical polymerization process, an anodic potential is applied to the monomer to oxidize onto the electrode, and it leads to starting the polymerization [130,147]. The electrochemical deposition of the polymer continues until the applied potential is stopped. This is an advantage if the desired polymer is a thin layer sensor, a film electrode or for use in nanotechnology, because the thickness of polymer film can be controlled by polymerization time. In addition, the morphology of polymer is always uniform. However, this is a disadvantage if the enlarged production scale is intended since the working electrode has a limited area [147].

The most common CPs can be synthesized by electrochemical polymerization as well as by synthesized chemical polymerization. For instance, PANI can be not only synthesized by the chemical polymerization but also produced by the electrochemical polymerization. A great number of papers have been reported investigating the effects of parameters of properties of electrochemically synthesized CPs, as follows [148–150]:
•
The polymerization condition: electrode material (i.e., graphite, carbons, platinum, gold, indium tin oxide glasses-ITO), temperature, solvent type and composition, counterion, and pH,
•
Polymerization time and the nature of doping anion.

The electrical and other properties of obtained PANI are different depending upon the synthesis method. Bhadra et al. studied the synthesis of PANI by both electrochemical and chemical polymerizations and the comparison of conductivity and solubility of resulted PANI [151]. The electrochemical polymerization was performed by mixing the 3.1mL of aniline and 20mL of HCl aqueous solution (1mol/L) in the electrochemical polymerization cell. A potential difference of 0.7V versus saturated calomel electrode across the electrodes was used for 2hours. The resultant PANI was collected, washed, and dried. Then it was treated with ammonia until the pH was neutral and redoped with p-toluenesulfonic acid. The PANI synthesized by electrochemical polymerization has higher solubility and lower conductivity than that of chemical-polymerized PANI. One of the most important other parameters affecting the properties of synthesized PANI is the solution pH, as mentioned above. It was experimentally shown that the oxidation of aniline is dependent upon the solutions pH [149]. They showed that aniline species reacts in solutions of pH <2, whereas nonprotonated aniline species participates at higher pH values.

Besides, the yield and conversion of the polymer synthesized depends on the electrochemical characteristics of polymerization. It was experimentally found that the PPy yield is higher, whereas the conversion is lower at higher monomer concentration [152]. Also, it was found that the applied voltage has a strong effect on both yield and conversion of the PPy synthesized by Maiti et al. During electrochemical polymerization of PPy, electrode material and buffer solution used has an intense effect on synthesis of the desired polymer. It was reported that using an aqueous solution with pH <4 is more convenient to have homogeneous and smooth PPy films and the best pH was determined as 2–3.5 values [153].

Photochemical polymerization method basically uses the photons to initiate the photochemical oxidation of the corresponding monomer in the presence of photoillumination [154]. A photosensitizer material is used to assist the polymerization. Photochemical polymerization method of CPs has already been carried out by using different photoreactive metal salts. It was reported that PPy was effectively photosynthesized with tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(II)-Ru(bpy)3 2+ in aqueous solution by photoirradiation [155]. Again, the photochemical polymerization of PPy was performed by using Cu(dpp)3 2+ (dpp: 2,9-diphenyl-l,10-phenanthrolin) under the 150W halogen lamp irradiation [156]. Recently, photopolymerization of PANI was performed by using only nitrate ions to initiate the polymerization of the aniline monomer under UV light [157]. One of the most important advantages of this method is the preparation of partially different conductivity by adjusting irradiation time. Also, this method can be easily used to have large and smooth CP patterns onto many different surfaces, such as ceramic, textile, paper, plastic, and wood [158].

Plasma polymerization of CPs is one of the most attractive alternative methods to improve the stability, flexibility, and transparency of CPs, which are always obstacles due to high reactivity of the conjugated backbone of polymer chain. Therefore, plasma polymerized CPs have been receiving interest and attention. In plasma polymerization, an alternative electric field is applied to a chosen gas and it initiates to create gas ions, molecules, and radicals in a high energetic state. This state is known as plasma. When plasma and the corresponding monomer collide, the activation of the monomer starts and activation of monomers leads to polymerization [159]. With this method, the modification of substrate surface is possible, while keeping the substrate bulk properties and transparency. In addition, the preparation of ultrathin, well adhering, and pin-hole free films of CPs for the use in electronic applications is easy by plasma polymerization [160].

Plasma polymerization is a dry method that does not require solvent and polymer can be deposit on almost any substrate, even onto porous surfaces [161]. The properties of plasma polymerized CPs can be easily adjusted by the controlling the plasma parameters, i.e., pressure, temperature, gas flowing rate or power [162]. However, the presence of many different reactive species (electrons, ions, radicals, etc.) is a disadvantage due to the different possibilities of multiple interactions of species. Consequently, it is hard to predict the exact chemical structure of the surface after exposure to a plasma [163].

The plasma polymerizations of many CPs have been already studied by scientists. Groenewoud et al. performed the pulsed plasma polymerization of PTh to obtain highly transparent layers [164]. They studied the effect of pressure, power, pulse time, cycle, and position in the reactor on the conductivity of the polymerize PTh and showed that the conductivities of PTh layers were higher when the pressure was higher. Dams et al. reported that the PEDOT films obtained by the plasma polymerization have low conductivities up to 1×10−2 S/cm [165]. The conductivity of plasma polymerized PANI from aniline monomer in the presence of iodine was reported as 2.3×10−5 S/cm [166].

Metathesis is a chemical reaction in which one part of each two compounds interchange to form two new compounds. Metathesis polymerization occurs according to this defining characteristic. Metathesis polymerization is different from other polymerization method, because all double bonds in the monomer remain in the polymer synthesized. This method is an outgrowth of the Ziegler–Natta polymerization and catalysts used are often the same or similar, such as a transition metal compound, and mostly are organometallic compounds [154]. In the literature, there are different methods to apply metathesis polymerization and the most popular ones are the ring-opening metathesis polymerization and ring-closing metathesis polymerization. Masuda et al. studied the properties of an acetylene-based conjugated polymer polymerized by metathesis. They successfully synthesized highly stable conjugated acetylene-based polymers with high molecular weight and solubility by a metathesis polymerization method [167].

Pyrolysis forms extended aromatic structures by eliminating heteroatoms (e.g., nitrogen, oxygen, or halogen) from polymer via heating [154]. In this method, the production of extended conjugation presumably increases the charge carrier mobilities and the forming of free radicals increases the number of charge carriers because these free radicals act as acceptors to form electron carriers (anions) or as donors to form hole carriers (cations) [168]. The pyrolysis conditions (final temperature, time, etc.) and the starting polymer have a strong effect on the resultant polymer form. The resultant polymer can be fiber, film, or powder depending on the effects of the parameters.

In inclusion polymerization, inclusion compounds initiate the chemical reaction, which proceeds in the molecular level spaces of the host compound where the monomeric guest components are polymerized [169]. Inclusion polymerization can be used to produce composite materials as well as stereoregular polymers [170]. Therefore, this method has a great potential to produce novel low-dimensionality composite polymer materials. Such polymer materials can show some characteristic electrical, magnetic, and optical properties in the inclusion state owing to their low-dimensionality [169]. For instance, the inclusion of an electrical conductive polymer might produce molecular wire. To this respect, inclusion polymerization has received more attention in the preparation of composite materials with different host compounds. There have been reports on the formation of such polymers with organic and inorganic hybrid hosts obtained by using zeolite, V2O5, MoS2, etc. [171,172].

Solid-state polymerization is a method in which the starting material is heated until the end groups mobilize enough to react in the absence of oxygen and water. This method is performed either by vacuum or by passing an inert gas to control the pressure and to remove the by-products [173]. Solid-state polymerized materials mostly have enhanced properties since the other side reactions can be reduced or even avoided owing to low operation temperature. The rheological, mechanical, and morphological properties of polymer can be adjusted by controlling the temperature, pressure, and the diffusion of by-products. The most used method is the after melt-polymerization prior to injection blow-molding to improve the physical properties of polymers [174]. Conductive polymers have been already prepared by solid-state polymerization by many researchers. A typical solid-state polymerization procedure for PANI was reported by Abdiryim et al., as follows [175]: 1mL of distilled monomer was added drop by drop into the 1mL of 37wt.% HCl solution at 0°C for 10minutes. 2.2g of ammonium peroxydisulfate was added into the mixture by grounding for 20minutes. The resultant polymer precipitate was filtered, washed, and dried under vacuum. To have different PANI salts doped with inorganic acids, the precipitate was treated with HCl, H2SO4, and H3PO4. The structural and electrical properties of doped PANI samples were characterized to determine the effect of acid treatment. Kim et al. studied the solid-state synthesis of a conductive polymer for careful control of crystal formation, because the electrical and optical properties of the produced CPs can be directly affected by crystal forms [176]. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) and poly(3,4-ethylenedithiathiophene) (PEDTT) was successfully synthesized by solid-state polymerization in air and under vacuum and their thermoelectric properties were examined by Chen et al. [177].

Since the preparation of submicron particles is possible by concentrated emulsion polymerization, this method is considered as an interesting topic in academic and industrial arenas. In the concentrated emulsion polymerization method, the main mechanism is the radical polymerization. The radical formed by decomposition of an initiator reacts with the monomer in a heterogeneous system. This method consists of the emulsification of monomer in water by an oil-in-water emulsifier and then, the initiation reaction starts with oil-soluble or water-soluble free radical initiator. The resultant is called latex, synthetic latex, or polymer dispersion and it is soluble either in the monomer or in the solvent [178,179]. This method is environmentally friendly, because the polymerization medium is generally water. The concentration of monomer and initiator, solvent, and temperature can influence the properties of emulsified polymer [179]. It was reported that the structural and electrical properties of concentrated emulsion polymerized PANI is affected by the water phase concentration by Pan et al. [180]. They showed that the prepared PANI has higher conductivity, when the volume fraction of water is about 20%–30%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Small Molecules, Their Properties, and Synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Organic small molecules, which is the other class of organic semiconductor materials, include the conjugated oligomers and fused ring molecules [181]. Small molecule semiconductors have been receiving growing attention due to their unique electrical and optical properties. With these materials, the production of new electronic devices with unique functionalities is possible [182]. In organic electronic devices, small molecules have some key advantages compared to CPs. The biggest obstacles related to preparation of CPs are the distribution of molecular weight and the reproducibility of the size, which bring an additional obstacle to the molecular design. In contrast to polymeric counterparts, there is no endgroup contamination in small molecules [183]. The optical, electrical, and structural properties of organic small molecules (neutral, charged, or metal complexes) can be adjusted with different chemical functionalizations [181]. Additionally, small molecules offer definite molecular weight, synthetical well reproducibility, monodispersity with well-defined chemical structure. In addition to these advantages, small molecules can self-assemble into ordered domains and improve the charge carrier mobilities. On the other hand, their thin-film assemblies can be easily broken even in the presence of orthogonal solvents since the weak intermolecular forces (e.g., van der Waals, π–π stacking, or hydrogen bonding) are effective attachments between each other [184]. The synthesis of small molecules usually depends on the procedures that are specified for each target [185]. Therefore, selected examples will be discussed in this part. The properties and application areas of some major classes of organic small molecules are given in Table 24.2 .

Oligothiophenes are one of the most studied organic semiconductor families due to their good charge transport properties and high polarizability. Additionally, they have adjustable electrical and optical properties [188]. The representative member in Table 24.2, known as sexithiophene, is the most studied and investigated oligomer and it shows a field effect mobility of ~0.03cm2/V/s in bottom-contact Si/SiO2 FETs [182]. Many members of oligothiophenes family are commercialized in industrial areas. These materials can be synthesized to use as semiconductors in organic electronic devices by well controlled different routes, resulting in monodispersity. Most common synthesis methods of oligothiophene are borrowed from synthesis methods of polythiophenes and their derivatives, but these methods result mostly in short oligothiophenes (>8 units) [189]. In brief, it can be said that there are three different routes for oligothiophenes synthesis, namely oxidative coupling, palladium-catalyzed C–H homocoupling, and palladium-catalyzed cross-coupling [189,190]. In oxidative coupling method, the precursor is oxidized with a certain oxidation agent or deprotonated, then the resultant is dimerized to form short α-conjugated oligomers [191]. Although this method provides regioirregularity and undesirable α-β couplings, it is simple and the coupling occurs at the highest electron density positions [192].

The palladium-catalyzed C–H homocoupling of bromothiophene method was developed as a new strategy to synthesize oligothiophenes with a silver reagent as an activator by Takahashi et al. [193]. They reported that this method results in in oligothiophenes bearing C−Br bonds at the terminal thiophene rings, which can be functionalized by catalysis. In the palladium-catalyzed cross-coupling method, many oligothiophenes can be synthesized based on halothiophenes. The most common catalysts used are palladium(II) acetate, Herrmann–Beller catalysts and tetrakis(triphenylphospine)palladium(0) [194]. With this method, monodispersity with isolation and purification is possible, but it requires number of synthetic steps [195].

Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons have an important role in chemistry and material science. Homologous classes of these materials such as acenes, phenacenes, and oligorylenes in crystalline state, are among the most promising materials as the channel materials for organic electronic devices. Especially, acenes are one of most studied classes and have shown some of the highest mobilities observed in organic semiconductors [187]. This class is defined as laterally fused benzene rigs with one Clar sextet [196]. The representative molecules in Table 24.2 are the most promising materials in organic electronics, especially in organic FETs (OFETs). Pentacene exhibits the high hole mobility since the laterally fused ring structure is convenient for π–π overlapping between the molecules [187]. However, pentacene has poor nature of solubility and it is sensitive to oxygen, therefore, it is limited in use in device applications. Herwig and Müllen proposed a method to synthesize soluble pentacene in 1999 [197]. There are many other different methods proposed to synthesize pentacene and its derivatives [198–200].

As another example of acenes, tetracene shows the edge-to-face orientation in solid state. Tetracene have been widely investigated especially in the configuration of OFET devices with large photosensitivity and a high hole mobility as 0.03cm2/Vs. Additionally, there have been reports that tetracene was used in solar cell application with other semiconductors paired [201]. Like the oligoacenes, poor solubility of tetracenes requires vapor growth methods in high vacuum. The high quality and defect-free crystals can be obtained by vapor growth method and it brings advantages to the evaluation of tetracenes in single-crystal devices. The functionalization of tetracenes may increase solubility and make them available for the low-cost solution deposition. Therefore, there are many different methods reported for the synthesis of tetracene and tetracene derivatives. Tulevsk et al. have reported the synthesis method and the study of FET application of 2,3-dihydroxytetracene [202]. They reported that the tetracene derivative monolayer attached to oxide surfaces as an active channel is a promising method to produce self-assembled electronic devices. Chen et al. have exhibited the synthesis route for a series of partially fluorinated tetracene derivatives [203]. They showed that the substitution of alkyl/alkoxy groups on the main chain improved the solubility, and also the crystal structures and electrochemical behaviors can be tuned by substitutes.

Perylene bisimides and their derivatives have been given a significant importance because of their unique chemical, photochemical, and thermal inertness and their potential to be applied in organic electronic devices. In addition to their high stability properties, their reversible redox and high molar absorptive properties are the other advantageous of these organic dye molecules [204]. Similar to other small molecules mentioned above, the electronic and solubility properties of perylene bisimides can be adjusted by the substituent of conjugated aromatic core. For the electronic device application, these materials can be prepared with either electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups. Weitz et al. synthesized different core-cyanated perylene carboxylic diimides functionalized with fluorine-containing linear and cyclic substituents and used the synthesized materials in OFETs [205]. There have been many reports about the other substituted perylene bisimides synthesized, such as alkoxy-substituted, alkyl-substituted, aryl-substituted, boryl-substituted, nitro-substituted etc., their properties and application areas [206–210]. Mikroyannidis et al. have reported the synthesis of perylene bisimide with acetonaphthopyrazine dicarbonitrile terminal moieties and the application of solar cells [211]. Although the nonsoluble perylene bisimides can be easily synthesized from perylene tetracarboxylic acid bisanhydride by reacting aliphatic or aromatic amines, the soluble perylene bisimides are more important for their use in low-cost applications. Generally, two main methods have been reported to enhance the solubility. The first one is substituting the imide nitrogen with solubilizing groups. The second one is introducing substitute groups at the bay-area by replacing bromine or chlorine [187].

Phthalocyanines are two-dimensional organic tetrabenzoporphyrin analogue compounds including four isoindole subunits attached by nitrogen atoms [212,213]. Unsubstituted and metalloderivatives of phthalocyanines are very important dyes and pigments due to their strong optical absorption in visible region. Most of metal elements can be used to form metalloderivatives of phthalocyanines. Consequently, this flexibility allows to tune their physical, optical, and electronic properties and to enhance the processability [212]. For example, their Cu(II), Co(II), Pt(II), Zn(II) complexes can sublime at 550–600°C in contrast to their Cd(II), Ca(II), or Mg(II) complexes [187]. Therefore, this small molecule group have been investigated widely as targets for LEDs, OFETs, solar cells, photosensitizers, sensors, etc. [213]. As one of the most investigated metalloderivatives of phthalocyanines, the copper phthalocyanines were mostly used in transistors owing to their high mobility. The mobility of single-crystal copper phthalocyanine was reported as 1cm2/Vs [181,214]. Although, many different approaches have been reported to synthesize unsubstituted and metalloderivatives of phthalocyanines, there are main routes used mostly [215]. For the synthesis of metal-free phthalocyanines and metalloderivatives of phthalocyanines, diiminoisoindole, 1,2-benzenedicarbonitrile, 2-cyanobenzamide, or 2-phthalic anhydride can be used as starting materials following by the treatment with acid or metal ions. Functionalization of phthalocyanines can be carried out by tailored synthesis from different precursors or by direct substitution of the core molecule. Even though most of phthalocyanines synthesized can be recrystallized from high-boiling solvent, they are insoluble at room temperature [187].

ce:section.ce:section-title: A General Overvıew of Nanocrystal and Organıc Molecules Applıcatıons in OPV, OFET, and OLEDs

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic optoelectronic devices based on silicon crystals are still the fundamental blocks of modern technology. However, their counterparts, organic-based devices have interacted after the Nobel-prize crowned work of Heeger, MacDiarmid, and Shirakava on molecularly doped polymers in the 1970s, because of the light weight, low cost, low temperature, large area, and mechanical flexibility applications. Especially, due to the rapid improvement of materials and processing, the technological usage of organic semiconductors became an attractive research area even though they have some drawbacks such as low reproducibility, stability, and charge carrier mobility. The employment of organic materials by organic electronic applications that can be considered as the foundation of organic electronics are organic photovoltaic cells (OPV), organic LEDs (OLED), and OFETs. In principle, organic electronics rely on electrically active materials that are based on conjugated organic compounds. Moreover, organic crystals with micro- and nano-scale have more advantages than large area counterparts in spite of conserving materials and saving time and also give rise to the efficient characterization of electronic devices. In this part, we focus on the organic molecules and NC device applications including charge transport properties, fabrication, and characterization in OPV, OLED, and OFET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic Semiconductors: Band Theory in Organic Chemistry
ce:section.ce:para: The origin of the semiconductor properties of both inorganic and organic materials come from the forbidden gap in their electronic structure. To figure out the chemical bonds and the electrical properties, we should focus on the interaction of a few metallic atoms. The atomic orbitals only interact with each other when two atoms are sufficiently close which means their wave functions (bonding-lower energy, antibonding-higher energy) interact as well. When the interaction occurred between n atoms, n very close energy levels are generated. On the other hand the highest energy level occupied by electrons in a metal is called the Fermi energy, Fermi level, or Fermi surface.

Organic semiconductors bear different properties from their inorganic counterparts because organic molecules create solids as a result of van der Waals interactions, whereas in inorganic solids, molecular bonds are covalent. Thus, the molecular interactions are not strong and the electrons have a free path almost comparable with intermolecular space. When the organic molecules come together, they have higher energies and create a much larger potential well than an isolated molecule has. On the molecule higher atomic orbitals work together to realize delocalized atomic orbitals, and also a deep atomic orbital is localized in the nucleus potential well. For further information we can separate these two energy levels as HOMO and LUMO levels. HOMO and LUMO levels have the same meanings, valance, and conduction bands in inorganic semiconductors, respectively. Since in organic semiconductors charges are localized at the molecules because of energetic and locational defects, in organic semiconductors charge transport is incoherent and also conductivity lower than inorganics. The charge transportation among the organic molecules appears by the oxide-reduction process.

Organic light-emitting diodes are known for short as OLEDs. OLED technology stands on the application of conjugated organic semiconductors, as thin film devices emit light once an electrical charge passes through when a potential applied. Within the last few years, OLED technology has become very popular for making large-screen displays and smart technologies such as phones and accessories. Another essential application of this technology can be found in the lighting market where it has a big potential to replace the traditional light sources as surface emitting sources.
In this part, we will briefly give the history and working principals of OLED, including fabrication and characterization methods and also favorable organic semiconductors for OLED applications.

After the eight years of the study of A. Bernanose on the electroluminescence of carbazole compounds, for the first time an organic molecule-based optoelectronic device was achieved by Pope in 1963 [216,217]. Unfortunately, this study couldn’t reach one step further than the laboratory level due to the produced devices not being stable or efficient enough to become commercial. In the early 1980s, to make more efficient OLED devices, researchers attempted to change the fabrication method to thin-film deposition using antracene molecules [218]. Nonetheless, device efficiency was low, too low to consider the commercial exploitation. It would take at least 10 years of the organic revolution for optoelectronics to emerge finally. In the late 1980s, a research group from Kodak company accomplished the first organic electroluminescent diode by evaporation of two organic thin layers [219]. The following device structure is still in use in some experiments today, ITO (indiumtinoxide)/diamine/AlQ3 (Aluminumtrisquinoline)/Mg/Ag. This achievement can be imagined as the beginning of the OLED technology. After that, in the following device structures, scientists have continued to use two main layers as hole and electron transport such as TPD and Alq3, respectively. The next big step in OLED technology came from a British research group. They fabricated an electroluminescent diode by using a conjugated polymer which shows ambipolar charge properties [220].

In a simple organic LED, whole device architectures can be fabricated by solution process or thermal evaporation techniques. Although small molecules can be deposited by both of these methods, conjugated polymers can be deposited only by solution process methods. The working steps of an electroluminescent device can be explained in a few steps. As a first step, charge carriers (electrons and holes) are injected from electrodes under an applied electrical potential to the transport layer. These transport layers also act as blocking layers of the opposite charge types. In the emission layer (inside the luminescent material) electrons and holes can be captured by coulombs interactions which are called excitons. Recombination of an electron and hole from an exciton cause the emission of electromagnetic radiation and in this way the molecule energetically turns back to its ground state as the last step (Fig. 24.10 ).

The emitted photon wavelength then depends on the material. For example, with AlQ3 green light around 520nm is emitted. OLED devices fabricated for large screens must provide three main colors (RGB—red green blue). On the other hand, lighting studies for industrial applications focused on white color organic LEDs. Usually white light can be obtained from the mixture of two or three main colors and that means we need to use at least two chromophoric materials as the emission layer on the same device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic Small Molecules and Polymers Semiconductor Material for OLED Applications
ce:section.ce:para: Material scientists have developed a large number of small molecules used in OLED technology. Some representative small molecules used in OLED can be seen in Fig. 24.11 .

Conjugated organic molecules can be classified by molecular weight as small molecules which have low molecular weight and polymers with bigger molecular weight. Opto-electronic properties of the conjugated organic molecules can be easily optimized by attaching different chemical side groups. In the application of these molecules thin-film fabrication methods include thermal evaporation or wet deposition techniques, which can be chosen according to the molecules’ solubility properties. In this field, there are different forms of conjugated polymers such as homopolymers or copolymers composed of an enormous number of monomers, oligomers, and dendrimers having a few monomers also used to fabricate OLEDs (Fig. 24.12 ).

Charge transport mechanism in small molecules and polymers are very similar because of the delocalization of the charges on HOMO and LUMO levels. Charge carrier in conjugated polymer can hop from a monomer to adjacent monomer caused by a weaker conductivity, which is called intermolecular conductivity, than in the small molecules.

Thin film quality is the one of the most important things in the whole OLED fabrication process. Prerequisite for thin-film deposition in OLED fabrication is to get uniform and pin-hole free morphology, also thin film thickness should be lower than 100nm. Conjugated polymers and small molecules have different difficulties in obtaining homogenous thin-film surfaces. Wet deposition techniques are suitable and inevitable for polymers since their huge molecular weight makes it impossible to evaporate thermally. Therefore, conjugated polymers must be well soluble in common organic solvents. However, a thin film of small molecules can be grown by wet deposition techniques and thermal evaporation techniques as well. These methods will be described below.

To produce an efficient OLED device, the purity of the organic materials is as important as producing homogenous thin films. To get electronic grade purity, which could be obtained by train sublimation for organic molecules, is necessary prior to organic electronic devices integration. The small molecules sublime under vacuum and travel down a temperature gradient and condense at different distances depending on molecular mass. Before thermal evaporation, small molecules must be purified with this technique to prevent impurities that may be detrimental for device performance. The purification process of small molecules is much easier and more achievable than for polymers. Polymers are not convenient for thermal evaporation or this purification method because of their nature. Although small molecules have unique properties for vacuum evaporation in OLEDs, they are not suitable for patterning by conventional lithography. Patterning is a prerequisite for fabricating large-screen display devices with OLEDs. In the vacuum evaporation method, shadow masks are employed to produce patterned modules from organic small molecules. Usually a thin metallic plate which has desired shapes is used as a shadow mask. Because the thermal evaporation process must be done in a vacuum chamber, size of the substrates is limited by the size of the chamber.

In industrial applications, wet deposition methods have great advantages such as low cost and they are independent from the size limitation of the substrate. However, the prominent handicap of this technique is the lack of the refinement of organic materials. Besides organic material purification issues, the limitation of the success of wet deposition is the requirement for excellent anhydrous solvents. One of the most popular techniques to grow polymer thin films is the spin coating. Unfortunately, spin coating is not suitable for large-scale fabrication. There are many different soluble process methods employed in industry to achieve large-scale production, such as imprinting or ink-jet printing technologies. Although these techniques have significant advantages, they are not good enough for microdisplay fabrication.

The device which converts the solar radiation into current is called a solar cell. We can separately examine solar cells as three broad classes: (1) nonorganic- or inorganic-based solar cells; (2) organic-based solar cells; (3) hybrid solar cells, which are made by the mixture of organic and inorganic materials. Though inorganic and hybrid solar cells are out of the scope for this part, brief information will be given.

Solar cells made from silicon or 3–5 compounds are usually called monocrystalline inorganic solar cells [221]. Due to the birth of the solar cell concept beginning with a crystal form of inorganic materials, this type of cell is referred to as the first generation. In spite of the cost of the single-crystal silicon cells being higher than the polycrystalline type, in practical use the efficiencies are almost equal. That is why polycrystalline type cells have been used in many different commercial areas, such as house production, space research, or military applications [222]. Besides all of the advantages of first generation solar cells, they have great stability and are commercially viable [223].

The main drawback of the first generation solar cell is the high fabrication cost caused by the requirement for high purified silicon [224]. Although many research groups have focused on the amorphous or thin-film silicon technologies to lower the cost over many years, they couldn’t reach the single crystal’s performance and stability. The main reason of the failure of these researches was the impurification of the materials [225].

Thin-film solar cells referred to as second-generation cells are usually fabricated by deposition of the inorganic material as a thin film on a conductive and transparent material such as glass. In this fabrication method, the inorganic semiconductor layer can be grown layer by layer depending on the desired thickness and cell structure. This type of solar cell is often used in the industrial applications that do not require performance but low cost such as calculators or decorative products. There many different types of second generation solar cells sold on the market, such as amorphous silicon, cadmium telluride, or copper indium gallium selenide [226]. Today according to the NREL solar cell efficiency chart CIGS and CdTe thin-film cells show over 22% efficiencies, whereas amorphous thin-film silicon cells show about 14%. Despite the fact that researchers have been working on these materials for over 40 years, the relatively very young sisters of the perovskite hybrid solar cells have almost reached their efficiency in only 4 years [227].

Organic solar cells are called third generation cells, not only because of the date they came into existence in the world of solar cells but also because of the materials used to fabricate them. From this point of view they can be examined in a few different topics such as dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), polymers and small molecules based cells, and as a consequence of the mixture of organic and inorganic materials hybrid solar cells.

After the first DSSCs was produced in 1972 with chlorophyll on zinc oxide, the DSSC concept showed great progress and in 1991 they reached over 7% efficiency [227]. The main idea behind this progress is the use of mesoporous titanium oxide TiO instead of ZnO [228]. After countless experiments and different dyes and electrolytes scientists reached 13% efficiency in this concept [228]. The working mechanism of DSSC starts with the absorption of a photon by an organic semiconductor dye molecule attached to the transparent oxide. Then the dye molecule rapidly gives the excited electron to the oxide layer which is called the cathode electrode. At the same time, a hole left from the excited electron with the help of the ion diffusion in the electrolyte, passes through to the anode electrode. By this mechanism the excited dye molecule regenerates and the whole process can start again.

Many different types of polymer solar cells can be fabricated depending on the cell structure. Although every structure has different advantages and disadvantages, most preferable types could be bulk heterojunction solar cells and inverted type solar cells. Except for the structures in polymer solar cells, a few fundamental types can be mentioned such as bilayer monolayer and tandem solar cells.

As the father of the organic solar cells the first produced type is the monolayer structure. A single semiconductor material sandwiched between two electrodes which have different work functions is enough to fabricate a monolayer organic solar cell. The main issues in this structure could be the lower absorption and transport problems. As a consequence of these problems in this type solar cells efficiencies usually cannot get beyond 1% [229]. After experiments with various materials, the solar cell efficiency record reached 2% in this field [230].

The difference between the monolayer and bilayer type is only the employment of two different types of thin film between two electrodes instead of the single semiconductor layer. Usually, tin oxide compounds-coated glass as the transparent conductive layers (TCO) are employed as the substrate in organic solar cells. Due to their low work function nature of the TCOs, the donor and acceptor layer are grown on the substrate. As long as the suitable materials are chosen in the view of work function, bilayer structures give more efficient results than the monolayer solar cells because of the effective absorption and transport properties [231].

In the world of organic solar cells one of the most successful device structures is the bulk heterojunction type solar cell [232]. The mixture of the donor and acceptor organic semiconductor materials is grown as a thin film. The combination of two different charge carriers drastically increases the interface area, as a result of this increased area increased exciton formation boosts the efficiency. Using different semiconducting materials but the same BHJ structure (as shown Fig. 24.13 ), thousands of papers have been published in the last two decades by scientists worldwide. Studies with novel materials such as PTB7 on this structure got over the 10% efficiency barrier in 2016 [233,234].

One of the most attractive architectures as a protective structure of solar cells can be shown to be the inverted type. Changing the position of the electrodes is enough to produce an inverted cell in an ordinary BHJ or bilayer type solar cell. Therefore, the acceptor molecules which are very sensitive to oxygen and humidity, such as fullerene derivatives, stay on the TCO side of the cell. In the solar cell market, the TCOs which are used as a substrate are usually deposited on the glass or PET. The inverted structures can provide better protection to the acceptor materials, which stay between TCO and hole transport material, from the detrimental substances in the air without any encapsulation. In recent studies on inverted type solar cells, the low work function metals are employed as an anode, whereas the high work function oxides are used as the cathode. Besides the protective structure, inverted type solar cells show pretty good efficiencies to 10% [236].

Although conventional OPV devices show excellent stability and performance in experiments, these devices have insufficient absorption in the visible spectrum, due to the nature of the materials used as donor and acceptor. One of the most bright ideas proposed to amend this problem is the production of different solar cells with absorption of various parts of the spectrum individually together in a device layer by layer. Today this type of solar cell is called a tandem or multistructured solar cell structure. Unfortunately this idea which is so good in theory, in practice has some efficiency and cost problems [237].

Conventional OPV devices have many significant advantages such as fabrication ease, low cost, or applicability on flexible surfaces. On the other hand they suffer from long-term stability and performance problems associated with absorption. Scientists have been looking to combine the organic solar cells with inorganic counterparts to rectify the deficiency of organic cells. An improved blend of these two types of solar cells with better morphology could result in hybrid solar cells with high efficiency and stability. Significant advances have been obtained with hybrid solar cells in recent years. Nevertheless hybrid cells efficiencies are lower than the organic solar cells such as DSSC or conventional BHJ types [238]. There is still room for improving the effectiveness of the hybrid cells by well optimizing the organic and inorganic materials and upgrading the device structures.

Today one of the most promising hybrid candidates against inorganic cells regarding efficiency is the perovskite-based solar cells. Although perovskite hybrid cells are relatively young in literature, there are many published papers in recent years and perovskite crystal structures have been very well known for decades [239]. Even though scientists have been working on this type solar cells for only a few years, the efficiency of perovskite solar cells already surpasses the amorphous silicon cells. The simplest case of the perovskite structure is that of a simple cubic cell with one ABX3 formula unit per unit cell. In this case the A ions are at the corners of the unit cell with the B ion at the center and the negative ions occupying the face-centered positions. The new Perovskite group materials showed broad application in the following areas: piezoelectric, thermoelectric, semiconducting, and superconducting equipments [240]. Although many people think that lead is detrimental to the environment, because of the superior efficiency and the ease of the production and low cost, lead halide perovskite solar cells are the most attractive subject for scientists in recent years.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fullerene as a Small Molecule-Based Solar Cells
ce:section.ce:para: Fullerene structures have spherical cage consisting of hexagonal and pentagonal rings. C-60 fullerene, which is made of 12 pentagonal and 20 hexagonal rings, is the most used fullerene in device applications. C70, C74, C76, C78, and C80 are some of the members of fullerene family that can be used in various applications [241].

Sariciftci et al. have demonstrated that fullerene (C60) is a very good electron acceptor for organic solar cell applications and made known the charge transport from the polymer to the C60 cage for the first time [242,243]. As a next step, Yu et al. reported a study based on a bulk heterojunction organic solar cell using PC61BM in 1995 [244]. In this study, the PC61BM and polymer blend solar cell results, including charge separation, carrier mobility, and energy conversion efficiency, have been shown. In 2002, flexible devices started to be fabricated with polymer solar cells [245]. In this new solar cell field, solvents came into prominence regarding the effects on film morphology, for instance, chlorobenzene-used OPV devices show PCE of 2.5% but toluene-used ones exhibit lower than 1%. Chlorobenzene-coated devices have exceedingly uniform surface morphology leading to less aggregation of PC61BM molecules and enhancing the charge transport [246].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modified PCBM Derivatives Used in OPV
ce:section.ce:para: In organic solar cell applications, pristine fullerenes demonstrate low power conversion efficiency because of solubility problems causing aggregation and poor absorption [242,243]. Yue et al. have shown that methane-fullerene (PC61BM) which is a soluble material can be used as an acceptor in OPV and it exhibited better device efficiency [244]. After that, PC61BM became a common acceptor for OPV devices regarding easy fabrication (solution-processable), high carrier mobility (electron), low LUMO levels, and stability. The power conversion efficiency of the solar cell devices can be found by measuring open circuit voltage VOC, short circuit current JSC, and fill factor FF. That is why, to increase the device efficiency, one of the things that could be helpful is to enhance energy level alignment which can improve the VOC in solar cells.

The PC61BM structure can be modified to get better LUMO energy levels and also better device efficiency. The most used fullerene derivatives are PC60BM and PC70BM ([6,6]-phenyl C71butyric acid methyl ester) in OPV applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic Molecules and Nanocrystal Applications in OFET

ce:section.ce:para: OFETs, require the following three components: semiconductor, dielectric (insulator), and contacts (gate, drain, and source). The particular arrangement of these components may vary, as displayed in Fig. 24.14 . In OFET fabrication, the thin semiconducting layer can be deposited with solution processes (spin coating, drop casting, etc.) or vacuum process (thermal evaporation). Semiconductor layer must be contacted with source/drain electrodes and the dielectric layer must be contacted with gate contacts so it is also called gate dielectric. Source/drain electrodes are always metal contacts, whereas gate electrodes can be a metal or a conducting polymer [247]. In a conventional OFET device, the positive or negative charges move through the channel in a parallel direction to the dielectric surface. The source/drain electrodes are operated for charge injection in applied voltage and afford entry to the channel. A thin charge layer occurs between the source and drain contacts with applied voltage and current starts to flow. Current can be changed by using voltage magnitude to the source and drain contacts [248,249]. In OFETs, conduction takes place at the surface since the channel thickness is lower than a few molecular layers [232,250,251].

The field effect mobility of the active layer can be extracted from the drain current at the linear or saturated operation regimes (Fig. 24.15 ). The difference between these areas comes from how homogeneous the productive potential is at the semiconductor–insulator interface. The voltage at the source will always be equal to V
G . At the drain electrode, the voltage is V
