On/off ratio is another important parameter in OFETs that can be obtained from the transfer curve for both regimes (linear/saturation) specifying the drain current on state and off state situation. On/off ratio must be as large as possible for good switching behavior in OFETs. Fig. 24.16 A and B shows typical transfer characteristics (i.e., the drain current versus gate-source voltage for constant drain voltages) of the same OFET device. Fig. 24.16C shows output characteristics, the linear regime at low Vds, and the saturation regime at high V
ds can be demonstrated obviously [254–256].

To fabricate a high-performance OFET device, organic semiconductors must provide the acceptable conditions related to charge injection and transport properties:
•
HOMO and LUMO levels of the molecules must be suitable for electron and hole injection under applied voltage.
•
the thin film must be defect free to prevent the charge carrier trapping that can be done in two ways: (1) long axis of organic molecules can be oriented parallel to the surface normal as charge transport takes place through the intermolecular π–π stacking direction; (2) the gap between the source and drain contacts must be covered homogeneously with the organic semiconductor crystalline domains [257].

In a working OFET device, the sign of the applied voltages, including gate and drain voltages, specifies the channel type: under positive voltage (V
G and V SD), the majority charge carriers are holes so the organic material is said to be p-type channel; under negative voltage (V
G and V SD), the majority charge carriers are electrons so the organic material is said to be p-type channel. If the OFET is working under negative and positive voltages, organic material is said to be ambipolar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge Transport Properties in Organic Semiconductors
ce:section.ce:para: Charge-carrier mobility is one of the most important figures of merit of organic semiconductors; it determines the performance of organic electronic devices such as FETs and solar cells. On the other hand, transport properties of organic semiconductors are much more limited than that of their inorganic counterparts. It is worth noting that even in the best organic transistors, charge transport is still controlled by the existence of chemical impurities and structural defects [258]. The exact nature of charge transport in organic semiconductors is still not entirely clear. However, it can be said that, a net distinction between disordered (i.e., amorphous polymers) and highly ordered semiconductors (i.e., organic single crystals) can be used to fabricate OFET.

Band transport points to the mechanism set in crystalline solids including the conductors and semiconductors. When the atoms interact with each other, energy levels become very close, almost undistinguishable, and energy bands occur in the solids. The likelihood of any particular band being filled is given by the Fermi–Dirac statistics. It is worth noting that this transport model is only suitable if the distance between two atoms is smaller than the mean-free path (the mean distance between two collisions) in the solid. However, it replaces a power rule, that is, μ ∝ T −n , where n > 0, so the decreasing temperature increases the mobility [259].

Charge transport in nonordered semiconductors, such as polymers, is usually defined as flying of charges by thermal activation along the distribution of localized states or shallow traps. To get a good performing device, the charge carrier transport mechanism occurs by hopping between these localized states, causing relatively low charge carrier mobility. Conwell and Mott have recommended that overcoming the energy between these two states could be done by catching or releasing one phonon [260,261]. As represented by Mott, in the system which has the constant DOS at low temperature, carriers hopping over long distances start to be energetically much more preferable than hopping to high energy levels. Thus, the conductivity (σ) becomes temperature dependent. This is the well-known variable-range hopping (VRH) model. The VRH model is where charges can hop a short distance with a high activation energy or a long distance with a small activation energy [262]. The Vissenberg–Matters model presumes that increasing charge carrier mobility in OFETs can be achieved with increasing gate voltage since the injected carriers move the lower-DOS of the organic semiconductor and then other charges in the injection layer will fill the DOS at higher energy levels. This dependence of the mobility on charge density and thus gate voltage has been observed for many disordered semiconductors, and the model proved to be very useful to model organic FETs [263–265].

It is worth noting that according to the experimental resultant, hopping transport seems to be not valid for highly ordered organic semiconductors such as rubrene, tetracene, and pentacene (Fig. 24.17 ). Also, the measurements on purified crystals which present high mobilities show that decreasing temperature increases the mobility which means in use band-like transport instead of hopping transport. However, at higher temperatures (above 150K) the average free path of charge carriers is found to be proportional to the crystal unit cell, which disproves delocalized transport [266,267].

Organic semiconductors usually have a large number of aromatic rings which contain varying single and double bonds that cause overlaying p orbitals forming π-bonds. In these bonds, electrons are shared between two adjacent atoms on the ring regarding delocalization of electrons in metals. π Electrons can be moved freely in organic compounds which leads to conducting of electricity and acting as semiconductors. The organic material which is used in the OFETs as a semiconductor layer can be linear, two-dimensional structures or planar conformation of aromatic rings [268].

The organic semiconductors can be small molecules such as rubrene, pentacene, etc. (Fig. 24.17) and conjugated polymers such as P3HT(poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl)) (Fig. 24.18 ) exhibiting different structures. Tetracene and pentacene, having very simple structures which consist of benzene rings, are the most favorable small organic molecules for p-channel OFET applications. They are well known and well-researched materials regarding fabrication and characterization of OFET. For example, tetracene thin films can be deposited through vacuum sublimation and obtained hole mobility is 0.15cm2/Vs [269], while by using pentacene in OFET device, which is deposited through thermal evaporation in the vacuum, the obtained hole mobility is 2.2cm2/Vs [270]. Pentacene hole mobility is higher than the hole mobility of tetracene small molecule since pentacene has a greater molecule length than tetracene meaning that tetracene cannot provide as great a distance for carrier movements as pentacene.

Linear thiophene oligomers and thiophene derivatives (Fig. 24.17) are some of other popular organic small molecules for OFET applications. Sexithiophene (α-6T) (Fig. 24.17) can be deposited by thermal evaporation technique as a semiconductor in OFETs and carrier mobility of α-6T is nearly 0.1cm2/Vs [271]. Also, other thiophene-based materials such as ethyl-substituted oligothiophene have been used in OFET applications and they show acceptable working behavior and extraordinarily high On/Off ratios. Some of the thiophene-benzene oligomers demonstrate charge carrier mobilities up to 0.42cm2/Vs [272].

In polymer-based OFET devices, poly (3-substituted-thiophene)s are some of the most used semiconductors because of their easily applicable nature, such as good solubility and solution-processability. P3HT is the first polymer which can be deposited by solution process and it has shown a comparable field effect mobility result in p-channel OFET applications with poly-thiophenes that is 0.0001cm2/Vs.

Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are trendy organic semiconductor materials used in device applications for a long time [273]. The first attempts using Pcs to fabricate OFET were reported in the late 1980s [274,275]. One of the famous Pcs is the copper(II) 1,2,3,4,8,9,10,11,15,16,17,18,22,23,24,25-hexadecafluoro-29H,31H-phthalocyanine (F16CuPc). As can be shown in Fig. 24.19 , it has the molecular cage structure and shows n-type channel characteristics in OFETs. These materials are stable until 400°C heating but they are susceptible to oxygen. Pcs can be deposited by evaporation under vacuum conditions as a semiconductor in OFET device and their mobility values are between 0.0001 and 0.01cm2/Vs [275–277]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of NCs in Optoelectronic devices
ce:section.ce:para: NCs are of significant interest for technological applications in science and industry. Also NCs synthesis is stable, controllable, and reproducible [278–280]. NCs have been a subject of intensive study due to their optical, chemical, electronic, and mechanical properties, which have been used widely in optoelectronics [278,281–286], photonics [279,287], bioimaging, as well as in medicines and hydrogen generation devices [288–291]. QDs devices can be processed by different techniques which can lower the cost of fabrication. The discussion on application of NCs with recent studies and results will be helpful, especially to a variety of scientific researchers [278,282,287,292–294].

Over the last two decades, due to features of semiconductor NCs such as narrow emission band, size-tunable color, high stability, high luminescence quantum efficiency, NCs, such as CdX-, ZnO-, silicon-, PbS-based, have been reported as efficient LEDs [287,292–295].

In 1994, the first QD-LED was reported by Colvin et al. On an ITO/CdSe/PPV/Mg device, with external quantum efficiency (EQE) lower than 0.01% [296] and the study made way for a new perspective for optoelectronic devices [284,296–298].

Since this pioneering work, various QD LEDs with different active layers and structure have been made [295–302]. So, by changing the applied voltage, it is possible to obtain multicolor emissions from the same QDs.

Core-shell QD of high fluorescence quantum yields have been used as electroluminescent materials in high-performance LEDs. Therefore, using core-shell QD significantly increases the EQE of QD LEDs [299,300]. In 1997, Schlamp et al. reported devices of CdSe/CdS core/shell QDs. They showed improved external quantum efficiencies of 0.22% at luminance of 600cd/m2 [300]. Also, CdSe/ZnS core/Shell QDs based LED have been obtained with EQE of 0.01% to 2.7% by some research groups [281,303–308].
In 2000, Gao et al. achieved external quantum efficiencies of 0.1% with CdTe QDs [309]. Since then, remarkable development has been achieved in the QD LEDs [303,304,307].

In 2012, an effective course was achieved for devices with an inverted structure [284,310]. These QLEDs, showed a record EQE 18% (which is close to the theoretical maximum of 20%) [284]. This type of architecture allows for obtaining efficiencies and brightnesses that are comparable to those of state-of-the-art OLEDs. The demonstrated record peak EQEs for QD-LEDs are 20.5% for red [311], 14.5% for green [312], and 12.2% for blue [297]. In the past a few years, dramatic progress has been reported for the efficiency of QD LEDs [295].

The IR emissive active layer, assembled from PbS QDs was sandwiched between the PEDOT:PSS and ZnO built using QDs which have external quantum efficiencies (2.0%) higher than the highest values reported by Sun et al. [313]. Significant improvement was achieved using core–shell structure with PbS–CdS QDs demonstrating an average EQE of 4.3%±0.3% [314].

Green et al. and Ng et al. have demonstrated Si-QDs LEDs with EQE up to 1% [315,316]. Recently, Gu et al. fabricated Si-QDs LEDs with much more efficiency by improving the hole transport using an interlayer between ITO and PEDOT:PSS [317]. They obtained a highest value EQE of ~2.4% [317].

Kwon et al. reported EL from an LED that used GQDs as a emissive layer embedded in a 4,4Abis(carbazol-9-yl)biphenyl [318]. They reported that EQE of GQD LED is 0.1% [318]. Kim et al. reported similar results using poly(9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK) [319]. In 2016, Kwon et al. also reported; using functionalized GQDs, they fabricated high color purity LEDs that release green, orange, and red. The EQE of 1.28% were recorded with their GQD LEDs. Further, those devices are the highest worth reported for Carbon-QDs LEDs [320]. Son et al. manifested white LEDs with EQE of 0.18% by using zinc oxide-graphene QDs [321]. Xu et al. manufactured GQD LEDs with synthesized GQDs. Also those QDs can be dissolved in organic solvents and redesigned the device structure to utilize PVK and 2,2A-(1,3-phenylene) (OXD-7). In this study, they obtained EQE of 1.2% [322].

Overall, QD-based LEDs currently demonstrate high performance and lifetime. Also QD LEDs are accessing the commercial area, so they can compete with OLEDs [287,292–295,323]. It depends on their ability in high performance, lifetime, low cost, large-scale manufacture, and flexible substrates which are the most important factors for improvement of QD LEDs-based display and lighting to compare with OLEDs [324].

Solar energy is one of the most important highlights for next-generation renewable energy [278,287,292–295,325,326]. NCs are of great attention because of their optoelectronic speciality promises for photovoltaic (PV) applications making low-cost and high-efficiency solar cells [278,325,326]. There are many advantages of using QDs as photoactive materials in solar cells. To absorb more photons from sunlight, QDs play important roles in absorption and high extinction coefficients. In recent years, there has been many studies and increasing efficiencies in solar cells [278].

In the last couple of decade of QDs solar cell research, different types of device structure have developed, such as Schottky junction solar cell, QD sensitized solar cell (QDSSC), Bulk Heterojunction solar cell (BHJ), as shown Fig. 24.20 .

A typical solar cell relies on a Schottky junction between a metal and a semiconductor. In this concept, QDs and low work function metals Al, Ag, or Mg are used. Schottky junction PV has a simple design for an amazing example of QD-PV devices. Schottky QD solar cells based on PbS, PbSe, and CdSe were achieved by Sargent and Nozik groups. They reported a lot of papers about explicator fabrication and operational fundamentals of Schottky PV devices with PbS [327–329] and PbSe [328,330,331]. Since the Sargent group first reported PCE of 1.8%, many studies have been carried out to improve the PCE, morphology, thickness, chemical treatments of NC solids, and stability. Also, many studies have been published using different materials, with low-work function metals such as Mg [328], Al [332,333], Ag [334], and Al/Ca [335] to optimize the cathode. Johnston et al. reported Schottky junction PV devices were prepared with PbS films [336]. These devices reached 4.2% power conversion efficiency. The most effective devices, which are PbS QDs restricted between ITO and LiF/Al electrodes, were fabricated by Piliego et al. [337]. They achieved a PCE of 5.2%. Furthermore, Olson et al. fabricated ultrathin PV CdTe film solar cells with a power conversion efficiency of 5.0% [338]. Pb-based QD-PV performance has led to certified device efficiencies of a remarkable 9.2% [339] a rapid rate of increase considering the sub-1% [340] of early reports. A 16.1% of PCE of CdTe film solar cells has been reported [341].

The DSSC was developed by Grätzel [342,343]. In this type solar cell, light is absorbed by the dye followed by an electron transfer from an excited state of the dye molecule into the conduction band of a wide band-gap semiconductors. QDs as compared to DSSC show superior photostability and wider absorption profile. In QD-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs), QDs replace the high-cost and unstable ruthenium(II)bipyridine dye and act as sensitizers to the TiO2 semiconductor layer. After the light is absorbed by a QD, creating an exciton that is dissociated at the QD/TiO2 interface, electrons are injected into the TiO2 conduction band and move to the TCE. After this, the QD is reduced by the electrolyte and the electrical circuit is closed due to reduction of the electrolyte at the counter electrode.

To passivate the surface, self-assembled QDs [344–347] were used to improve the efficiency of PV cell. Plass et al. demonstrated a high surface area heterojunction between TiO2 and a spiro-OMeTAD was sensitized to visible light by PbS QDs [348]. The sensitized heterojunction showed energy conversion efficiencies of 0.49%.

Kamat et al. assembled CdSe QDs onto the surface of mesoscopic TiO2 films [346]. The carrier injection from the excited state of CdSe QDs into TiO2, which can be used to generate photocurrent, was confirmed.

Niitsoo et al. deposited CdS QDs onto porous TiO2 films and treated the electrode by direct deposition of CdSe or Cd-rich CdS layer to study the influence of deposition parameters on the performance of the QD-SSC [345]. They reported that the existence of Cd-rich CdS layer prior to CdSe deposition can improve the energy conversion efficiency to as high as 2.8%. Lin et al. used a similar method and they achieved an efficiency of 1.35% [344]. Diguna et al. demonstrated high-efficiency CdSe-TiO2 QD-SSCs by modifying the surface of CdSe QDs with ZnS and fluoride ions [349]. The energy conversion efficiency reached as high as 2.7% under solar illumination of 100mW/cm2. Kongkanand et al. demonstrated different-size CdSe QDs assembled on TiO2 nanotube. They observed the maximum power-conversion efficiency ≤1% achieved with CdSe−TiO2 film [350]. Also, Peng et al., reported CdS QDs sensitized TiO2 nanotube-array photoelectrodes [351]. The CdS QD–TiO2 nanotube structure enhances the charge transport in solar cells and significantly improves the energy conversion efficiency to higher than 4.15% under AM 1.5G illumination.

Im et al. demonstrated PV cells where PbS QDs were deposited by spin coating technique on mesoporous TiO2 [352]. The device structure was FTO/bl-TiO2/mp-TiO2/PbS CQD/P3HT/PEDOT:PSS/Au. Under standard conditions, they achived an overall energy conversion efficiency of 2.9% [347]. Baker and Kamat reported TiO2 films treated with CdS QDs [353]. Pure CdS-solar cell reached about 1.6% power conversion efficiency. However, CdS coated with CdSe reached 4.2% [353]. Santra and Kamat investigated Mn2+ doping CdS-based QDSSCs. Mn doped-CdS/CdSe semiconductor deposited on TiO2 and doping the CdS in the CdS/CdSe system with Mn2+ ions enhanced the power conversion efficiency to 5.4% [354].

Recently, ZnS or ZnSe passivation layers have shown a high of PCE of CdS/CdSe-based QDSSCs [355,356]. Huang et al. investigated the characterization of the electrochemical and optical properties of ZnS and ZnSe as a passivation layer and their performance of QDSCs. The energy conversion efficiency reaches up to 6.4%, for the solar cells with a ZnS-QDs and 3.4% for the solar cells without ZnS-QDs. In this work it was demonstrated that ZnSe would be a good alternative to ZnS as a passivation material [355,356].

Zhao et al. achieved a PCE of 8.21% (8.55% for a champion cell) using ZnS/SiO2 double layer for CdSe x Te1−x QDSSCs [357]. Ren et al. demonstrated the amorphous TiO2 layer can efficiently enhance the performance of a ZnS/SiO2 layer and reached PCE of 9.28% for a CdSe0.65Te0.35 QDSSCs [358]. Zhou et al. using (Pb,Cd)S/CdS as a hybrid structure, reached an effective power conversion efficiency up to 4.08%, better than that of the conventional PbS/CdS pattern (2.95%) [359].

The design of a hybrid PV devices are connected with the combination of QDs and conductive polymers. For the last two decades QDs have been used with these polymers. QDs and the polymer can be assigned in absorption and exciton formation in a QD–polymer hybrid PV cell. Different QDs semiconductors such as PbS, PbSe, CdS, CdSe, ZnO, etc., and conductive polymers, such as P3HT, PCBM, MEH-PPV etc., were examined.

Wang and Herron reported using synthesized CdS in poly(vinyl-carbazole) matrix [360] to improve the photoconductivity effect of QDs. Greenham et al. achieved the energy conversion efficiency of 0.1% [361] with CdSe QD in MEH-PPV polymer based on inorganic–organic hybrid solar cell. The energy conversion efficiency was 0.1% [361]. To improve the device performance using CdSe in P3HT [362], demonstrated by Huynh et al., the CdSe nanorod/P3HT blend resulted in a power conversion efficiency of 1.7%. Also ZnO QDs-hybrid solar cells has an energy conversion efficiency of 0.9% and 1.6% for P3HT and MDMO-PPV, respectively. [363]. Watt et al., reported PV devices using PbS NCs in conducting polymer MEH:PV. These devices have white light power conversion efficiencies between 0.7% and 1.1% [364]

Noone et al. studied BHJ solar cells containing blends of colloidal PbS QDs with several new donor–acceptor polymers like PDTPOx, MDMO-PPV, and P3HT [365]. PV devices made with PDTPOx/PbS blend resulted in PCE larger than previously studied BHJ blend. Sun et al. used different polymers in the polymer/PbSe, PCE of 5.31% was achieved [366].

Yuan et al. compared the effect of polymer structures with new polymers based on DTP and PbS-QDs size. They achieved a PCE of 4.23% as high as [367]. CdSe based on hybrid solar cells and a fluorene and thiophene units into PCBM blend were investigated by De Freitas. The CdSe/PFT devices showed a PCE of 1.3% [368]. Khan et al. demonstrated the impact on PV performance of P3HT on doping of CdS QDs. These devices have good efficiency of the solar cell from 0.45% to 0.87% [369]. Recently Wang et al. manufactured a device by inserting a TBAI-treated PbS QDs. PCE reached 3.02% with the heterojunction organic solar cell [370].

Charge transport measurements on QDs in FETs have shown rather limited ability until recently, largely as a result of poor carrier mobilities due to the choice of interparticle ligand materials. For FET devices the basic requirement is that good quality thin films can be formed from the QDs solution. This requirement is readily suitable for spin coating layer by layer QD deposition methods. For the fabrication of a QD FET, generally two configurations are used. For the bottom-gate configuration, a highly doped silicon wafer is used as a gate separated from the QD film with a dielectric. On this substrate two electrodes, source and drain, are patterned by lithography and evaporation of a metal. The QD film is deposited by drop casting or spin coating. For the top-gate configuration, after the deposition of electrodes and QD films, a dielectric layer is formed on top. This dielectric layer separates the top gate from the QD film [323].

Using pyridine-capped CdSe colloidal semiconductor QDs, the first inorganic FETs fabricated were reported by Jacobson et al. [371]. They annealed the nanoparticle layer above 250°C allowed sintering of QD into film. The device reported by Jacobson et al. can’t be defined as QDs FETs as there were no QDs were existing in the FET. The device shows an I on/I off ratio of 3.1×l04: the mobility of 1cm2/Vs, a V th of 6.7V, and a SS of –7 to 10V/decade. The carrier mobility in these QD FETs showed up significantly higher than compared to printed organic TFTs.

Recently high-mobility QD FETs were reported. Choi et al. demonstrated CdSe QDs thin-films field effect mobilities for electrons of 27cm2/Vs at room temperature [372]. Also, Oh et al. achieved high mobilities of 10cm2/Vs in PbSe QDs FET which is greater than previously reported PbSe devices [373].

Chung et al. reported an impressive electron mobility of 38cm2/Vs in CdSe QD FET without compromising low operation voltage and hysteresis [374]. Also, recently studies shows that, despite starting from extremely low conductivities in the early works, in 2017 mobilities higher than 2700cm2/Vs have been achieved in QD FETs. Ghimire et al. demonstrated a hybrid FET with a graphene and CdSe. The graphene is known to play the role of an electrode that conducts the photoexcited carriers from the CdSe to the electrodes. They achieved field effect mobilities for electrons of 2700cm2/Vs [375].

Recently many researchers have been developing neuromorphic electronics based on Au nanoparticle high-performance nanoparticles organic memory FET (NOMFET), that use as biological synapses, with reliable low-power consumption, data storage, and low-manufacturing cost [376–383]. This device was designed to mimic the human synapse. NOMFETs works on the principle based on the charge trapping/detrapping in an array of gold Nanoparticles at the SiO2/pentacene interface [377].

ZnO QDs shows very interesting material properties for FET devices [384–388]. ZnO has some advantages like low cost, nontoxicity, chemical stability, etc. Sun and Sirringhaus prepared NCs-based FETs from ZnO [388]. The mobilities of ZnO TFTs were μ lin 2.4×10−4 cm2/Vs and μ sat 4.6×10−4 cm2/Vs, and the I on/I off ratio was 5×103. These FET device performances could be enhanced by using ZnO nanorods.

QD FETs will doubtlessly continue to develop with colloidal QD films for other devices like solar cells and light-emitting devices. Due to colloidal QDs properties for optoelectronic applications and the impressive improvements in the last few years, colloidal QD FETs will continue from research driven by scientific curiosity to economically attractive commercial applications in the near future.
